title: The trigger of unexpected physical contact
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The touch that wasn't yours to choose

I was standing in line at a coffee shop. Just standing there, minding my own business, staring at my phone. And then it happened. A hand landed on my shoulder. Lightly. Probably meant to be friendly. Probably meant as a "hey, I know you" kind of thing.

My body went cold. Then hot. My chest tightened. My jaw locked. I couldn't speak. I couldn't move. I just stood there, frozen, while someone I vaguely recognized from somewhere chatted at me about something I couldn't hear over the roaring in my ears.

They meant nothing by it. That's the thing. They probably don't even remember doing it. But I remember. My body remembers. And it's still sitting in my nervous system somewhere, that moment of unexpected contact, that violation of my physical boundary that I never agreed to.

Here's the thing about unexpected physical contact. It doesn't matter if it's "just" a tap on the shoulder. It doesn't matter if it's "just" someone grabbing your arm to get your attention. It doesn't matter if it's "just" a hug from a relative at a family gathering. Your nervous system doesn't know the difference between "just" and "danger." Not when you've been through trauma. Not when your body has learned that touch can mean threat.

Think about it. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about something called neuroception. That's your body's automatic scanning system for safety and danger. It happens below conscious awareness. You don't decide to feel safe or threatened. Your nervous system decides for you, based on cues it has learned over a lifetime.

And if you grew up in an environment where touch was unpredictable, where it was used to control you, where it came with strings attached or pain attached, your neuroception learned something specific. It learned that unexpected touch is a red alert. It learned that you cannot trust the hands that reach for you.

I'm not talking about obvious abuse here. I'm talking about the subtle stuff. The parent who grabbed your arm too hard when you were trying to walk away. The relative who insisted on hugging you even when you pulled back. The friend who poked you as a joke even when you said stop. The partner who touched you in your sleep without asking.

These moments accumulate. They build a database in your nervous system. And that database tells your body one thing: touch that I did not initiate is not safe. Touch that I did not consent to is a threat.

And here's where it gets really hard. Because most people don't understand this. Most people live in bodies that didn't learn that lesson. They assume touch is friendly. They assume physical contact is connection. And when you flinch, or freeze, or pull away, they take it personally. They think you're rejecting them. They think you're cold or weird or unfriendly.

You're not. You're just trying to survive a world that keeps touching you without asking.

Why your body reacts before your mind can catch up

Let me tell you what's actually happening inside your body when someone touches you without warning. Because it's not just "being sensitive" or "overreacting." It's a biological process that has real, measurable effects.

When unexpected touch happens, your nervous system goes through a sequence. First, there's the orienting response. Your head turns. Your eyes widen. Your body goes still. This is your system trying to figure out: what is this? Is this safe? Is this dangerous? Do I need to fight, flee, or freeze?

Then, if your system decides this is not safe, you get the full threat response. Your heart rate spikes. Your breathing gets shallow. Your muscles tense. Your digestion shuts down. Your pain perception changes. Your brain starts releasing stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline.

This happens in milliseconds. Before you can think. Before you can remind yourself that this is just your aunt or your coworker or your friend. Your body has already decided: danger. And now you're in survival mode.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how traumatic experiences are not stored as stories in the narrative part of your brain. They're stored as sensations, as physical responses, as body memories. Your body remembers what happened even when your mind tries to forget.

So when someone touches you unexpectedly, your body isn't just reacting to this moment. It's reacting to every moment of unwanted touch you've ever experienced. It's reacting to the accumulation. It's reacting to the history that your nervous system has been tracking, whether you're consciously aware of it or not.

And here's the part that makes me angry. The wellness industry loves to tell you that you need to "heal your boundaries" or "learn to say no" or "practice self-love." As if the problem is that you're not trying hard enough. As if you just need to affirm your way out of a nervous system that has been trained for years to protect you.

That's not how it works. That's not how any of this works. You can't think your way out of a biological threat response. You can't positive-affirmation your way out of a freeze state. Your body needs something different. It needs safety. Real safety. The kind of safety that comes from being with people who actually respect your physical autonomy.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work is deeply grounded in the neuroscience of how trauma creates parts of us that hold different responses. It helps explain why one part of you might want connection while another part flinches at touch.

The shame spiral that follows

Here's what happens next, and this is the part that breaks my heart. After the physical reaction comes the shame. You flinched. You pulled away. You said something awkward or nothing at all. And now you're sitting with the aftermath, replaying it in your mind, telling yourself that you're broken, that you're too much, that you're not enough.

You might apologize. You might overexplain. You might try to cover up your reaction by forcing yourself to tolerate the touch even though every cell in your body is screaming. You might dissociate, just float away somewhere, because that feels safer than being present in your body while someone is touching you without your consent.

And then you spend the rest of the day exhausted. Because maintaining that kind of hypervigilance takes energy. Because fighting your own nervous system takes energy. Because the shame takes energy.

I want to say something directly to you. Your reaction is not a character flaw. It is not a sign that you are broken beyond repair. It is not evidence that something is wrong with you. Your reaction is a sign that your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's protecting you. It's keeping you safe. It's using every piece of information it has learned from your life to make the best guess about what you need right now.

Your body is not the enemy. It's trying to help you. Even when it feels like it's betraying you, even when it feels like it's making your life harder, your body is on your side. It's just using outdated information. It's operating from a database that was built in a different time, in a different context, when the threats were real and immediate.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). It helps make sense of why your boundaries were never respected and how that shaped your nervous system's expectations about touch and connection.

Stay with me here. Because I know this is uncomfortable. I know you might be reading this and feeling that familiar tightness in your chest. That's okay. That's normal. That's your system doing its job. Just notice it. Don't try to change it. Don't try to fix it. Just let it be there.

What actually helps (and what doesn't)

So what do you do when unexpected touch is a trigger? What do you do when you can't control the world around you, when people are going to keep touching you without asking, when you have to a society that doesn't understand body autonomy?

First, stop trying to fix your reaction. Stop trying to make yourself less sensitive. Stop trying to "get over it." Your sensitivity is not the problem. The problem is that you've been living in a world that doesn't respect your boundaries. The problem is that you've been taught to override your own body's signals in order to make other people comfortable.

Second, start practicing micro-boundaries. These are small, low-stakes moments where you assert your physical autonomy. You don't have to make a big scene. You don't have to explain yourself. You just have to say one thing: "Please don't touch me." Or "I don't like being touched without warning." Or even just holding up your hand as a signal.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You (paid link). Mark Wolynn's work explores how trauma can be passed down through generations, which might help explain why your nervous system responds the way it does even if you don't have a clear memory of trauma yourself.

Third, build relationships with people who actually respect your body. This is harder than it sounds. It might mean having uncomfortable conversations with friends or family members. It might mean creating distance from people who don't respect your no. It might mean spending more time alone while you figure out what safety actually feels like in your body.

Fourth, work with your body directly. Not through thinking. Not through analyzing. But through gentle, somatic practices that help your nervous system learn that touch can be safe. This might mean working with a trauma-informed therapist who does somatic work. It might mean practicing self-touch, putting your own hand on your own arm, your own chest, your own face, and noticing what that feels like. It might mean getting a weighted blanket or a body pillow that provides pressure without surprise.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link). Jonice Webb's work on childhood emotional neglect helps explain why you might have learned to disconnect from your body and its signals in the first place.

Fifth, and this is the hardest one, give yourself permission to be "rude." I put that in quotes because it's not actually rude to protect your body. But we've been so conditioned to prioritize other people's comfort over our own safety that setting a boundary can feel like a violation. It's not. It's survival. And you get to survive.

Look, I'm not going to tell you that this gets easy. I'm not going to tell you that one day you'll suddenly love being touched by strangers or that you'll never flinch again. That's not realistic. That's not how healing works. Healing is not about becoming someone who doesn't have triggers. Healing is about becoming someone who can be with their triggers without falling apart. Healing is about learning to trust your own body again, even when it's scared. Healing is about finding people who will wait for your yes instead of taking your silence as permission.

You are not too sensitive. You are not broken. You are a person with a history that lives in your body. And your body is doing its best to keep you alive in a world that keeps reaching for you without asking.

That's not a problem to be solved. That's a reality to be honored.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link)
  • It Didn't Start with You (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
